data.title: U.S. IPO Weekly Recap: Sizable Insurance IPOs Deliver Solid Trading
data.description:
A few new issuers went public this past week, joined by two SPACs.

Bermuda-based Aspen Insurance (AHL) upsized its share offering and priced at the midpoint to raise $398 million at a $2.8 billion market cap. Aspen is a specialty (re)insurer with a diverse product mix balanced across its primary specialty insurance and opportunistic reinsurance franchises. The company grew GWP in 2024 and delivered solid return on equity. Aspen finished up 15%.

Florida-focused insurer American Integrity (AII) priced at the midpoint to raise $110 million at a $313 million market cap. Through its insurance carrier subsidiary, American Integrity provides a variety of residential property insurance policies. The company has established itself as a key player in the Florida residential insurance market. American Integrity finished up 8%.

While not included below, drug developer Apimeds Pharmaceuticals US (APUS) priced at the low end to raise $14 million at a $47 million market cap. The company is developing Apitox, an intradermally administered bee venom-based toxin, to treat inflammation and pain management symptoms associated with knee osteoarthritis. Apimeds US finished down 45%.

4 IPOs During the Week of May 5th, 2025

One sizable filer joined the pipeline this past week. Chronic care management platform Omada Health (OMDA) filed to raise $100 million.

Blank check company targeting businesses in the APAC and EMEA regions.

Week Ahead

Three IPOs are scheduled to list in the week ahead, although some small deals may join the calendar at the last minute.

Global trading platform eToro Group (ETOR) plans to raise $480 million at a $4.5 billion market cap. eToro’s platform aims to combine a social network with the ability to trade and invest, all within a regulated, digital investment platform for financial discourse. As of December 31, 2024, eToro had approximately 3.5 million Funded Accounts across its global footprint of 75 countries. The company’s total commission grew 45% in 2024, and its net contribution grew 41%. However, it is dependent on volatile trading volumes and prices, and is subject to varying regulations, especially within the crypto space.

Singapore-based oil and gas equipment maker OMS Energy Technologies (OMSE) plans to raise $50 million at a $399 million market cap. The company manufactures surface wellhead systems and oil country tubular goods, primarily for onshore and offshore E&P activities. OMS has a 10-year agreement with Saudi Aramco, which accounts for a majority of the company’s revenue.

Singapore-based digital asset-focused financing firm Antalpha Platform Holding (ANTA) plans to raise $46 million at a $309 million market cap. Antalpha provides financing, technology, and risk management solutions to the digital asset industry. The company is the primary lending partner for Bitmain and provides supply chain financing solutions to institutional and corporate participants in the Bitcoin mining industry, offering loans secured by Bitcoin and Bitcoin mining machines. Its revenue jumped more than three times in 2024, although it demands on volatile crypto demand and prices.

IPO Market Snapshot

The Renaissance IPO Indices are market cap weighted baskets of newly public companies. As of 5/8/2025, the Renaissance IPO Index was down 6.1% year-to-date, while the S&P 500 was down 3.3%. Renaissance Capital's IPO ETF (IPO) tracks the index, and top ETF holdings include Kenvue (KVUE) and Arm Holdings (ARM). The Renaissance International IPO Index was up 9.7% year-to-date, while the ACWX was up 10.5%. Renaissance Capital’s International IPO ETF (IPOS) tracks the index, and top ETF holdings include Galderma and Porsche.

The comments below are not regulated with the same rigor as the rest of the site, and this is an 'enter at your own risk' area as discussion can get very heated. If you can't stand the heat... you know what they say...

Moderation Guidelines:

We remove comments under the following categories:

Regardless of which side of the political divide you find yourself, please be courteous and don't direct abuse at other users.

data.title: Pool Corp.: Overvalued Given No Recovery Is In Sight
data.description:
Shares of Pool Corp. (NASDAQ:POOL) have been steadily declining since late 2024, as an elevated multiple and increased concerns about consumer discretionary spending have weighed on shares. Recent results have done little to allay these fears, and tariffs may further threaten the pace of consumer spending. I last covered POOL in June 2024, reiterating the stock as a “sell.” Since then, shares are basically flat, so while there was no urgency to sell in hindsight, there was no reason to continue holding either. I continue to view shares as significantly overvalued.

In the company’s first quarter reported on April 24th, POOL earned $1.32, which missed estimates by $0.14, as net sales declined by 4% to $1.1 billion. EPS was down 29% from last year as the decline in sales drove a pretty meaningful loss in operating leverage. I would note that this quarter did not have a leap year, so sales were down 2% on a selling-day basis. Overall, maintenance activity remains solid while new construction is pretty weak.

Ultimately, maintenance activity should prove more recession resistant than construction. While some maintenance activity can be deferred for a time, chemical treatments and equipment replacements are inevitable. Conversely, based on consumers’ view of the economy, it is easy to defer the decision over whether to build a new pool. Importantly, over 60% of the business is maintenance related. This helps to dampen the extent of POOL’s exposure to the economy, though it still is a cyclical business.

This bifurcation in the business is evident when looking at end market results. Chemical sales held in flat while building materials were down most sharply. Interestingly, its lower-cost Pinch a Penny franchises are somewhat outperforming retail as a whole, which speaks to a more cautious consumer. Remember, Q1 also was prior to most tariff announcements, which if anything, have further dampened consumer sentiment.

In Q1, gross profit fell by 8% to $312 million margins compressed 100bs to 29.2%. New construction is generally higher margin, so the relative outperformance of maintenance work will apply pressure on margins consistently in my view this year. Plus on the earnings call, management noted double-digit growth in private label chemicals. While chemical purchases are nondiscretionary, it does appear consumers are “trading down” to manage their spending, which could pressure margins. Overall, while revenue fell by $49 million, costs were down by just $23 million.

Even with top-line pressure, SG&A was up 2% as POOL continues to invest in digital offerings and faces ongoing wage inflation. If we see a more notable turndown in economic activity, I would look for management to more aggressively manage costs. Importantly, inventory was down by 2% to $1.5 billion. It spent last year working through a glut of product, and it is now positioned better, which should allow it to avoid discounting.

Operating expenses were 21.9% of sales, up 140bps from last year. As a result, operating income fell by 29% to $78 million as margins compressed 250bps to 7.2%. POOL generated $27 million of operating cash flow, but there was a $68 million tax payment for 2024 taxes. It spent $13 million cap-ex and returned about $100 million to shareholders, including a $56 million buyback. Its share count is down about 2% from last year.

I would note that POOL maintains a strong balance sheet, enabling it to return capital even during a significant downcycle. It carries just $1 billion of debt, and it has 1.5x debt to EBITDA, up slightly from 1.4x last year. Given the nature of the business, I would be comfortable with leverage running up to 2x. On April 30th, the company announced a $600 million share repurchase program, doubling its existing size, and increased its dividend by 4% to $1.25. This gives shares a ~1.7% dividend yield. I would note there is no end-date to the buyback program, and I would not expect the entire amount to be used over the next year, instead targeting $200-300 million.

Looking forward, the company did reiterate guidance for $11.10-$11.60 in EPS, including a $0.10 one-time tax benefit. There were some signs the business was nearing a bottom during Q1. For instance, revenue grew in March after being particularly weak in January and February, when the weather was particularly weak. Outside of Texas, which is very soft, it is seeing stabilization in its key markets of Florida, California, and Arizona. We are seeing higher-income consumers hold in better than the middle class as entry-level pool demand is soft, with the high-end holding in better.

Absent some acceleration in newbuilds, I believe it will be difficult to achieve the top end of its gross margin and earnings expectation. Management also expects to pass on tariff costs, which primarily impact equipment costs. It sees a 2% impact from tariffs. It raised equipment prices in April and expects to do so again in June. I am cautious about its ability to offset all tariff costs, but the ultimate impact here is fairly small. Based on its backlog, there should be revenue growth in Q2, but the outlook from there is uncertain.

My view has been that the US can avoid recession, but that economic growth is likely to be below 1%. Given how much uncertainty there is, consumers on the fence about a big purchase are likely to delay, especially with home prices in the Sun Belt softening, which may reduce the willingness to upgrade. As a share of GDP, spending on residential improvements is below its COVID peak, but it remains quite healthy, actually.

In other words, the level of spending we are seeing is not recessionary. Rather, there is still some overhang from a pull-forward in demand during and immediately after COVID. As such, I see residential improvement spending growing no faster than GDP, and potentially a bit slower over the next 18 months. This is likely to limit the potential growth in newbuild activity. I would expect spending to be flattish over the next 18 months.

Given my view that newbuild activity is likely to stay soft while maintenance should be stable, I expect earnings of $11.00-$11.30 this year, excluding tax benefits. That leaves shares at 27x earnings. While POOL has a dominant position in the industry, this is an extremely elevated valuation, suggesting that earnings will sharply rebound. Frankly, I do not see a sharp rebound at least over the next 18 months, given an aged economic cycle, incremental inflation pressures, and a healthy level of residential improvement spending today.

Last year, I argued POOL should trade at 16-17x earnings, based on a 10x multiple for new construction activity and 20x for more steady maintenance work. To the extent that newbuild spending is running below normal, there is an argument for a higher multiple, as earnings today are relatively depressed. This is why cyclical stocks often have multiples expand when earnings fall sharply and trade at lower multiples near peak earnings. Still, I struggle to see an argument for this company trading past 20x, as there is no likely to be a meaningful rebound in newbuild activity over the next 18 months.

As such, $225 continues to be the highest fair value I can ascribe. Shares of POOL have been held up by the hope of a sharp eventual rebound, which caused shares to rally in H2 2024. As that has not happened, the stock has come right back down. I believe hopes of a cyclical turn are likely to lead to ongoing disappointment, driving further underperformance. As such, I remain a seller and view POOL as materially overvalued.

data.title: The Fed's Dilemma: Waiting For Hard Data Amid Political Pressure
data.description:
By Christian Floro, CFA, CMT, Market Strategist

The Fed has emphasized the need to wait and see if the weakness in soft data translates into weakness in hard data. They are beholden to trade policy - itself incredibly uncertain - and are now likely to delay further rate cuts until late Q3.

Also complicating matters is the ongoing challenges to their independence, which has the potential to undermine monetary policy credibility. In the meantime, investors should prepare for a higher-volatility environment marked by fragile rate expectations, increased market sensitivity to Fed signals, and renewed pressure on the U.S. dollar.

With uncertainty extremely elevated, and with risks of a stagflationary environment - higher unemployment and higher inflation - high as well, the Federal Reserve has been plunged into an almost impossible situation.

The Fed has emphasized the need to wait and see if the weakness in soft data translates into weakness in hard data. Although they are beholden to fiscal and trade policy, it’s unclear whether the current level of tariffs will be sustained, and to what extent they may evolve in the coming months as potential trade deals emerge.

In this situation, it’s likely the Fed sits on its hands. Rate cuts will be required but, increasingly, they will need to wait until late Q3 before the window of opportunity opens, starting with a 25bps cut in September, followed by another in October and then in December.

However, complicating matters, the Fed’s wait-and-see approach potentially opens them to renewed criticism from the Trump administration. Ongoing political jawboning and challenges to Fed independence could undermine the credibility of future monetary policy choices. The Fed’s anchoring effect on interest rates may also be challenged if it is seen as succumbing to the President’s directives.

As a result, while easier monetary policy is likely to emerge eventually, investors should also be prepared to see more bouts of volatility, with longer-term yields and the dollar particularly vulnerable.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Introducing The S&P 500 Market Leaders Index
data.description:
By Elizabeth Bebb

The S&P 500® Market Leaders Index is designed to track companies within the S&P 500 that hold significant market share and sustainable competitive advantages. Unlike the broader S&P 500, this index zeroes in on companies that consistently outperform their peers in terms of return on invested capital (ROIC), free cash flow (FCF) generation and substantial market presence. In this blog, we will delve into how this index is constructed, examine its back-tested performance and highlight its unique characteristics.

Methodology Overview

The S&P 500 Market Leaders Index selects companies using a Market Leader Score based on the average of three key metrics.

  1. High Market Share: This metric may indicate competitive advantages like economies of scale, network effects or strong brand power. Companies with high market share often have better pricing power and higher returns.
  2. Sustained High ROIC: Calculated as the five-year average of net income divided by total equity plus total debt, this metric assesses profitability and reflects a company’s efficiency in capital utilization. Market leaders often exhibit stronger ROIC due to their dominant market positions.
  3. Consistent High FCF Margin: Companies that maintain consistently high FCF margins over five years, combined with high ROIC, tend to be able to effectively redeploy cash flow, helping them maintain market share.

The top 50 companies ranked by Market Leader Score are included in the index and are weighted by float-adjusted market capitalization (FMC).

Historical Performance Characteristics

The S&P 500 Market Leaders Index has demonstrated impressive long-term performance compared to the benchmark. As shown in the back-tested period covered in Exhibit 2, it has outperformed with lower volatility, leading to better risk-adjusted returns. The index not only offered greater upside potential but also a defensive stance. Notably, during the recent tariff-related drawdowns, the S&P 500 Market Leaders Index outperformed the benchmark by 2.23%.

Historical Drawdowns

The S&P 500 Market Leaders Index has often benefited from downside protection during past market sell-offs.

Higher Quality Constituents

Historical fundamental analysis of the index reveals a greater focus on profitability metrics that align closely with the quality factor.

Sector weights have been diversified and historically skewed toward Consumer Staples, Health Care and Information Technology compared to the benchmark, with underweights in Energy and Industrials. As of Dec. 31, 2024, the index showed overweights in Consumer Staples, Financials and Information Technology, while Health Care was underweight.

Historical Macroeconomic Performance

The S&P 500 Market Leaders Index tended to outperform the benchmark in most market environments but had the highest performance in periods of falling growth, regardless of inflation.

Conclusion

The S&P 500 Market Leaders Index tracks 50 of the most consistently high-performing companies in the U.S. equity market. By focusing on ROIC, FCF margin and market share, the index seeks to identify market leaders with sustainable competitive advantages. Historically, this approach has identified higher-quality companies that exhibited defensive qualities and strong risk-adjusted performance.

1 For more information, please see the full methodology.

2 Further explanation of the macroeconomic analysis is available in the blog A Historical Perspective on Factor Index Performance across Macroeconomic Cycles.

Disclosure: Copyright © 2025 S&P Dow Jones Indices LLC, a division of S&P Global. All rights reserved. This material is reproduced with the prior written consent of S&P DJI. For more information on S&P DJI, please visit S&P Dow Jones Indices. For full terms of use and disclosures, please visit www.spdji.com/terms-of-use.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Tariff Tango Keeps Feet Still
data.description:
Stocks tiptoed through Friday with all the urgency of a trade negotiator on lunch break. Hints of tariff relief danced through the headlines, but with more hemming than harmony, investors kept their feet mostly still.

Level Change 5/9/25 (%)

-0.3 Dow

+0.0 Nasdaq

-0.1 Nasdaq 100

-0.1 S&P 500

-0.1 S&P 400

+0.0 S&P 600

After two weeks of climbing on cautious optimism, the market decided to pause and ask whether the good news was real, or just lightly disguised hope. So far, the latter.

Friday brought more talk of US-China tariff “de-escalation,” though the president shifted his preferred rate from a rumored 54% to a firm-sounding 80%, which is de-escalation only if you’re grading on a Himalayan curve.

Still, the idea that tariffs might one day go from punishing to merely painful kept investors from bolting. Unfortunately, valuations already reflect a sunnier outcome. The S&P 500 is back to 21x forward earnings, which assumes double-digit growth through political crosswinds, supply chain snarls, and consumers who increasingly shop with their sighs.

The Fed, meanwhile, held its fire - and its script. The Fedspeak posse rode out Friday, each talking-point gunslinger sounding off in a slightly different register of “not yet.”

Governor Kugler pointed to economic resilience as a reason to wait. Governor Barr warned that tariffs could push inflation and unemployment higher, and not in a way that makes decision-making any easier.

New York’s Williams delivered the high-school lesson: without price stability, there’s no economic stability. And Richmond’s Barkin reminded analysts that retailers can’t pass on costs to consumers who’ve already passed out.

In short: the Fed is in wait-and-see mode, a position that sounds to those hankering for a preemptive rate cut like nap-and-mutter.

Among individual stocks, Nvidia (NVDA -0.6%) made headlines for the wrong reasons - again - by prepping a weaker chip to dodge US restrictions and maintain its China presence. Taiwan Semi (TSM +0.7%), by contrast, flexed with a 50% revenue jump in April, as clients rushed to hoard chips before tariffs make them a luxury good.

In the real economy, Expedia (EXPE -7.3%) cut guidance and dimmed the lights on US travel demand, while Lyft (LYFT +28.1%) soared on a better quarter and the novel theory that tariff-bloated car prices might drive more people into rideshares. Why buy a $40,000 car when you can get chauffeured around by someone else’s student loan debt for $12?

It’s a fair point - and one the Fed may ponder, once it finishes pondering the last thing it pondered.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: CTO Realty Q1: High Quality REIT With Growth Potential
data.description:
I rate CTO Realty Growth, Inc. (NYSE: NYSE:CTO) a Buy, for income focused investors interested in real estate investment trusts. The REIT pays a quarterly dividend yielding 8.6% and goes ex-dividend on approximately 6/13/2025. CTO is benefiting from a robust economy in the U.S., and its proven management team is achieving results. In the recent Q1 earnings call, management confirmed robust leasing activity and strategic acquisitions to grow the portfolio. The portfolio is now 93.8% leased, which increases the ability to collect rent and highlights the ability of management to maintain the properties.

The most important factors that influence my opinion of the stock are

  • Professional, proven management team with history of high performance
  • Quality properties with high quality tenants such as Whole Foods, Dicks Sporting Goods, and The Capital Grille restraint chain
  • Growing rentable square footage by 19% CAGR
  • Growing Enterprise Value by 26% CAGR.

Seeking Alpha Quant rates the REIT a Hold 3.11 (as of 5/9/25), earning an A for valuation and growth. This is most likely driven by the market volatility driven by the tariff announcement on April 2nd, 2025. The market reacted, causing price pressure downward and prices are still being tested with day-to-day announcements.

Retail Based Property REIT

Headquartered in Winter Park, Florida, CTO Realty Growth, Inc. is a publicly traded real estate investment trust that owns and operates a portfolio of high-quality, retail-based properties located primarily in higher growth markets in the United States. CTO also externally manages and owns a meaningful interest in Alpine Income Property Trust, Inc. (NYSE: PINE), a publicly traded net lease REIT.

The shopping center REIT is focused on open-air centers in fast growing metropolitan statistical areas (MSAs) within Georgia, Texas, Florida, and North Carolina. Tenants such as Whole Foods Market as seen in the chart below sign long-term leases on high quality properties.

CTO’s portfolio has 5.2 million rentable square feet with an implied cap rate of 7.9%. This square footage has been built up over the years as the management team selects new properties and disposes of unwanted properties in an effort to grow and upgrade the holdings. A cap rate of almost 8% is very healthy in my view.

As you can see from the pictures below, CTO has a portfolio that attracts high quality tenants such as Publix, PNC Bank, The Capital Grille restaurant, Dicks Sporting Goods, and Nordstrom Rack.

Q1 2025 Earnings Beat

On May 1, the company declared Q1 2025 earnings, with the following financial highlights:

  • EPS achieve $0.01 which beats estimates by $0.27.
  • Revenue of $35.81 million increased 27.3% year on year, beat estimates by $556.3K
  • Portfolio was 93.8% leased and 91% occupied.
  • Core FFO was $14.4 million, up $3.7 million vs. quarter year ago.
  • Net debt to EBITDA was 6.6x, with $140 million in liquidity at the end of the quarter.

Overall, this was a healthy quarter demonstrating management’s ability to strategically add square footage to the portfolio and manage what it has to be fully leased. They worked to re-lease the space after some tenants leave and finally, CTO maintains a financially healthy balance sheet with ample liquidity at the end of the quarter. In my view, this management team stands out as being highly professional, active asset managers. They are generating revenues and profits while building for the future.

Performance To Date

$10,000 invested in CTO over the past 5 years would have doubled, growing to $23,040. This outperformed the sector index using Vanguard’s Real Estate Index Fund ETF (VNQ) as a proxy.

Valuation

CTO is increasing in value over time as shown in the chart below. Enterprise value is estimated at $1.3 billion in Q1 2025. This represents a 26% compound annual growth rate (CAGR) since Q4 2020, which is robust in my view. They are continually making strategic acquisitions, which increases the square footage of available rental space. From Q4 2020, CTO has increased their portfolio from 2.5 million square feet to 5.2 million in 2025.

CTO also points out the REIT has an attractive valuation and compelling dividend yield. From the chart below, its peers average annualized dividend yield is 4.6% vs. CTO at 7.9%. Similarly, CTO’s estimated 2025 core funds from operations (FFO) multiple is lower than the majority of peers at 10.5x.

The peers listed in the chart above include Acadia Realty Trust (AKR), Federal Realty Investment Trust (FRT), Whitestone REIT (WSR), Urban Edge Properties (UE), Brixmor Property Group Inc. (BRX), Kite Realty Group Trust (KRG), American Assets Trust Inc. (AAT).

Outlook

The future for CTO is especially bright now. Interest rates are at a level where they can adequately finance properties to build square footage and their balance sheet is healthy, generating positive FFO, which drives stock value.

On the Q1 Earnings call, management reaffirmed its full-year 2025 core FFO guidance of $1.80 to $1.86 and AFFO guidance of $1.93 to $1.98. The company expects its leasing activity will have a net positive effect as a growth driver for 2026. CTO continues to focus on strategic acquisitions in their targeted MSAs in southeast and southwest of the U.S. Management highlighted the attractive pipeline of opportunities in the current environment.

Risks To Rating

One of the main risks to retail space is the financial health of the tenant. If they go bankrupt, the landlords need to spend Capex to release the space.

While there may be risks to certain tenants because of U.S. tariff policies, CTO is positioned as having high quality properties and a diversified tenant base. This means if some of the tenants are affected, it won’t affect all of them. Also, if those particular tenants need to leave, CTO will be able to release the space because it is desirable.

Bottom Line

I rate CTO a Buy, for income focused investors interested in real estate investment trusts. The high-quality portfolio of retail shopping centers in the southeast and southwest of the US attracts high quality tenants such as Whole Foods, Dicks Sporting Goods, and The Capital Grille restaurant.

CTO currently pays a quarterly dividend yielding 8.6% and goes ex-dividend on approximately 6/13/2025. Management reaffirmed its 2025 guidance on the latest Q1 earnings call beating earnings estimates. The current portfolio is 93.8% leased, which is highly encouraging for rent collection. CTO also discussed strategic acquisitions being closed in the quarter and also projected throughout the year which will impact growth for 2026.

Overall, the most important factors that influence my opinion of the REIT are a professional management team, proven strategy, and a healthy financial status. The team is growing rentable square footage at a rate of 19% per year, which helps to drive enterprise value of 26% per year.

Risks to the REIT include tenant financial status and the impact of tariffs on some tenants. However, the portfolio of tenants is diversified as is the locations of properties in the various MSAs.
